exemplar.title: A Comparative Analysis of Little Women In Literature and Film

detailedExemplarData.title: A Comparative Analysis of Little Women In Literature and Film

subject.title: English A Lang & Lit
subject.description:
**IB English A Language & Literature** is a **Group 1** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **the study of language and literature through a wide variety of texts and media from different periods, styles, and cultures**, with a strong focus on **critical analysis of both literary and non-literary texts, examining how language choices create meaning and effect in different contexts**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Paper 1** | Text analysis | 1h 15m | 2h 15m | 35% (SL), 25% (HL) |
| **Paper 2** | Comparative essay | 1h 45m | 1h 45m | 35% (SL), 25% (HL)  |
| **HL Essay** | Written assignment | N/A | 1200-1500 words | 25% (HL only) |
| **Individual Oral** | Oral presentation | 15 minutes | 15 minutes | 30% (SL), 20% (HL) |

strengths: Clearly communicates the topic and purpose of the study in the introduction
Research question is stated, consistently referenced, and appropriate for the chosen texts
Employs a recognizable comparative methodology with relevant primary and secondary sources
weaknesses: Lacks a precise definition of what constitutes a âtriumph of feminism,â which weakens the focus
Methodology section is not explicitly outlined (e.g. scene-selection criteria, theoretical framework application)
Introduction sometimes drifts into background material that dilutes the central research focus
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands for Focus and Method fell squarely into the 3â4 band. The investigationâs purpose and research question are clear and appropriate, and the methodology is generally relevant, but the student never defines key evaluative criteria or explicitly outlines their method. A score of 4 reflects an adequate but not yet exemplary articulation of focus and method.

strengths: Effective incorporation of Butlerâs gender performativity and other feminist concepts
Accurate and mostly consistent use of film and literary terminology (e.g. ânonlinear temporal structure,â âdenouementâ)
Coherent understanding of both primary texts, with appropriate citations
weaknesses: Oversimplifies the chronology of feminist waves, missing key historical nuances
Occasional narrative retelling of plot points rather than application of quotation to analysis
Relies on a relatively narrow range of secondary criticism, limiting depth of scholarly engagement
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both strands in Knowledge and Understanding were rated in the 3â4 band. The student demonstrates solid command of terminology and source material and integrates theory competently, but the breadth of sources is limited and there are occasional factual imprecisions. A score of 4 captures this generally good but not fully sophisticated performance.

strengths: Insightful analysis of Gerwigâs opening scene and its feminist framing
Compelling case study of Amyâs parallel scenes with effective application of Federiciâs feminist economics
Powerful reading of Joâs monologue that captures thematic tension between independence and vulnerability
Well-argued discussion of Gerwigâs sidelining of Friedrich to highlight themes of self-determination
Logical and coherent comparative evaluation in the conclusion, synthesizing key insights
weaknesses: Certain paragraphs lean more on descriptive summary than on critical evaluation
Counter-perspectives and evaluation of secondary critics are not fully explored
Connections between theoretical frameworks and narrative structure could be made more explicit in places
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although initial strand ratings sat in the 4â6 band, the number and quality of insightful analytical momentsâbalanced by only occasional lapses into summaryâpush the work into the 7â9 band for Critical Thinking. The student demonstrates good, sustained argumentation, predominantly supported by relevant evidence and critical evaluation. A score of 9 recognizes this strong performance while acknowledging the gaps in counter-argument and integration.

strengths: All required structural elements are present (title page, table of contents, clear section headings, page numbers)
Consistent application of MLA formatting in text and works-cited list
Readable layout with appropriate font size and spacing
weaknesses: Inconsistent indentation in the table of contents hierarchy
Citation of film timestamps lacks full MLA consistency
Minor punctuation and spacing errors in extended quotations and Works Cited heading
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation clearly meets all IB structural and formatting requirements with only minor lapses. Both strands fell into the 3â4 band, and a top-band score of 4 appropriately reflects an effective but not flawless presentation.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student employs a recognizable comparative literary-film methodology, drawing on primary texts, critical theory (Butler, Federici), and scholarly articles.  Source selection is generally relevant and referenced through in-text citation and a works-cited list, indicating informed choices.  Nevertheless, the essay lacks an explicit explanation of method (e.g., criteria for scene selection, rationale for theoretical lenses) and relies heavily on a limited range of secondary criticism, so completeness and sophistication are moderate rather than comprehensive, meriting the 3-4 band.

Layout conventionsâ12-pt font, double spacing, accurate in-text citations, page numbering, word count declaration, and correctly formatted MLA works-citedâare all in place.  Minor inconsistencies in citation style (e.g., time-coded film references without standard format) and occasional typographical slips are evident, but overall formatting meets IB expectations clearly and effectively, justifying placement in the 3-4 band.

The essay draws on primary evidence from both texts and incorporates a range of secondary sources (scholarly articles, feminist theorists, reviews).  Most sources are relevant to the research question and are generally integrated to advance the argument.  Nonetheless, the quantity and diversity of academic criticism are moderate, with occasional reliance on journalistic material.  The overall research base supports the investigation adequately, matching the middle of the 4-6 band.

The title, introduction and early paragraphs make the purpose of the study clear: a comparison of feminist achievement in Alcottâs novel and Gerwigâs film.  The student signals the primary focus (feminist triumph "in their times") and outlines the main analytical angles (narrative structure, dialogue, monologues).  While the topic is certainly identifiable and generally well framed, some phrasing is loose (e.g. âa triumph of feminismâ is never explicitly defined) and the introduction drifts into background material that partially dilutes the central focus.  These lapses keep the communication at an adequate rather than exemplary level, consistent with the 3-4 band.

The student sustains a coherent comparative discussion and attempts evaluationârecognising contextual constraints on Alcott and contrasting them with Gerwigâs freedoms.  Reasoned conclusions are offered in the final paragraphs.  Yet the argument occasionally loses precision, and evaluative comments are sometimes asserted without full consideration of counter-perspectives (e.g., limited challenge to the binary of âtriumphâ vs. âshortcomingâ).  The discussion is thus competent and occasionally insightful, aligning with the 4-6 band.

The analysis compares specific scenes and passages to feminist theory, demonstrating logical reasoning and some insight (e.g., juxtaposing Amyâs monologue with economic critique).  Claims are usually supported with textual or cinematic evidence, and quotations are explained.  However, sections drift into descriptive summary, and some assertions about âtriumph of feminismâ are stated rather than critically interrogated.  The depth is therefore good but not consistently probing, suiting the middle of the 4-6 band.

The essay follows the required structure: title page with key information, abstract-style introduction, clear section headings, table of contents, numbered pages, and a complete works-cited list.  Transitions between major sections are generally logical, though some internal paragraphing is uneven.  All mandatory elements are present and arranged coherently, satisfying the criteria for the 3-4 band.

The student demonstrates solid knowledge of both the novel and film, accurately summarising key plot points and historical context, and integrates feminist theorists and critics to support claims.  Citations are provided and generally appropriate.  Yet the discussion sometimes slips into lengthy narrative retelling and relies on a small pool of secondary sources, limiting the breadth of literary-critical engagement.  The understanding is coherent but not consistently sophisticated, aligning with the 3-4 band.

Throughout the essay the student employs subject-specific vocabularyâe.g., ânon-linear temporal structure,â âframing,â âfeminist economics,â âgender performativity.â  Terminology is mostly accurate and used in context, demonstrating awareness of literary and filmic concepts.  Occasional imprecision (confusion of âfirst waveâ chronology, informal phrases such as ârotted for the Joâ) and some syntactic lapses inhibit complete consistency, placing performance firmly in the 3-4 band.

The research question is clearly stated on the title page and repeated in the opening section; it is appropriate for an English A: Lang & Lit Category 3 essay and is consistently referenced throughout the discussion of narrative structure, dialogue, and feminist theory.  However, the wording (âto what extentâ¦a triumph of feminism in their timesâ) remains somewhat broad, and the essay never fully specifies criteria for judging a âtriumph,â so the degree of focus weakens in places.  The connection between question and analysis is sound but not always tight, fitting the descriptors for the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: This paragraph foregrounds plot summary over critical evaluation; refocusing on the implications of Joâs marriage choice would enhance analytical depth.
The discussion of Gerwigâs sidelining of Friedrich is wellâargued and contributes to the thesis by highlighting self-determination themes.
The inclusion of an accurate word count at the beginning satisfies the IB formatting requirement and demonstrates attention to detail.
The table of contents is clearly organized with appropriate section headings and page numbers, enhancing navigability and overall structure.
The analysis of Gerwigâs opening scene is insightful and well-supported by specific descriptions, effectively illustrating the filmâs feminist framing.
The indentation of sub-sections in the table of contents appears inconsistent; standardizing levels of hierarchy would improve visual clarity and coherence.
The overview of feminist wave chronology oversimplifies historical nuances. Incorporating precise dates and distinguishing movements by key milestones would demonstrate deeper subject knowledge.
The quotation from Federici is presented without sufficient critical engagement; the student should explicitly connect this theory back to comparative scenes to support the overall argument.
The transition into the methodological approach is abrupt. It would benefit the reader to include an explicit statement outlining the steps taken to select scenes and apply theoretical lenses.
Joâs monologue analysis powerfully captures emotional nuance and illustrates the tension between independence and vulnerability, strengthening the essayâs critical argument.
The incorporation of Butlerâs concept of gender performativity is effective and demonstrates solid understanding of relevant feminist theory.
The summary of the novelâs linear plot is descriptive; interrogating how this structure reflects period-specific constraints on female agency would deepen the critical analysis.
The title is clearly stated at the start of the report, meeting the formatting requirement and providing immediate focus for the investigation.

The research question uses the phrase âtriumph of feminismâ without defining what criteria constitute a âtriumph,â which weakens the focus. The student should specify evaluation criteria to tighten the research questionâs scope.
Works cited entries lack hanging indents and consistent formatting; applying MLA indentation and uniform styling will improve the reference listâs professionalism.

The introduction restates the research question but lacks a clear outline of the methodology. Including a brief explanation of chosen analytical frameworks or scene selection criteria would strengthen the method section.
The student accurately employs film terminology such as âexpositionâ and âdenouement,â demonstrating command of key concepts in narrative analysis.
The conclusion effectively synthesizes key insights but could be expanded to reflect on the broader implications for contemporary feminist discourse beyond the texts studied.
Citation of film timestamps lacks MLA consistency; applying a standardized in-text format for audio-visual material would improve presentation.
Reliance on criticsâ interpretations is descriptive; the student should evaluate these perspectives critically and consider counterarguments to deepen the analysis.
The terminology ânonlinear temporal narrative structureâ is used accurately and consistently, reinforcing the studentâs command of analytical concepts.
The Federici citation lacks consistency with works-cited details; ensuring matching bibliographic formatting for all sources will enhance credibility and presentation.
The extended monologue quotation is effective, but the student should balance direct quotes with analytical commentary to avoid overwhelming the reader with raw text.
Comparing Friedrichâs film portrayal with the novel would benefit from direct quotations from Alcott to clarify the contrast in character dynamics.
The connection between narrative structure and feminist theory could be elaborated by linking this device to the broader theoretical framework introduced earlier.
The case study of Amyâs parallel scenes is compelling, demonstrating effective use of filmic evidence to support the feminist argument.
The application of Federiciâs feminist economics to Amyâs character provides a nuanced critique of economic dependence and enriches the comparative discussion.
The analysis of Megâs dialogue choice effectively illustrates the theme of agency and aligns with the central feminist argument.
Time codes for monologues are presented without proper format; adhering to a consistent timestamp citation style will improve the scholarly rigor of the presentation.
The lack of closing quotation punctuation and a clear citation for this extended quote reduces clarity; precise punctuation and citations are essential for reader comprehension.
The âWorks Citedâ heading should follow standard MLA formatting by centering the heading and ensuring appropriate spacing before the entries.
